original_hypothesis.title: executeWithdrawals Strips Hook ExtraData, Bypassing Hook Access Control
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The batch executeWithdrawals function passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize to _executeWithdrawal, which is then forwarded to hooks.onExecuteWithdrawal. Since calldatasize() == msg.data.length, extraCalldataBytes is always 0. In contrast, the single executeWithdrawal uses a fixed 0x44, allowing appended calldata to pass through as extraData. If a hooks contract uses extraData for authorization (credential proofs, signatures, access tokens), the batch version completely bypasses this check by always passing empty extraData.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code-level inconsistency between two public entry points that perform the same operation. If hooks rely on extraData for access control (which the protocol explicitly supports via the extraData pattern across all hooks), the batch version silently strips this data, creating an authorization bypass.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeWithdrawals passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize causing extraCalldataBytes=0 in hook call, vs executeWithdrawal which passes 0x44 allowing extraData passthrough

preconditions: Market deployed with useOnExecuteWithdrawal hook enabled
Hooks contract uses extraData parameter for authorization or access control
Lender has a pending expired withdrawal batch with claimable balance

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls executeWithdrawals instead of executeWithdrawal to avoid providing extraData (authorization proof) to hook
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can execute withdrawals for accounts that should be restricted by hook-based access control, extracting funds that should be locked

economic_questions: Do any deployed hooks templates use extraData in onExecuteWithdrawal for access control?
Can an unauthorized caller exploit this to execute withdrawals for restricted accounts?
What is the total value at risk across markets using extraData-dependent hooks?
validation_requirements: Review all hooks template implementations for extraData usage in onExecuteWithdrawal
Test that executeWithdrawals always passes empty extraData to hook
Verify whether hooks can distinguish batch vs single execution calls

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to full withdrawal amount of restricted accounts

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After batch expiry when target account has claimable balance
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, direct value extraction

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol/lenders who rely on hook-based access control for withdrawal execution
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full withdrawal amount could be claimed by/for unauthorized parties
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All funds in withdrawal batches for markets with extraData-dependent hooks

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare baseCalldataSize in executeWithdrawal (0x44) vs executeWithdrawals (msg.data.length)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm extraCalldataBytes = calldatasize() - msg.data.length = 0 always holds for batch version

validation_result.mechanism: extraData stripping in batch executeWithdrawals bypasses hook authorization
validation_result.impact_summary: The batch executeWithdrawals function always passes empty extraData to the onExecuteWithdrawal hook by using msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize, creating a bypass for any hook-based access control that relies on extraData (signatures, credentials, access tokens).
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The single executeWithdrawal passes _runtimeConstant(0x44) as baseCalldataSize, which is the size of the standard calldata (4-byte selector + 32-byte address + 32-byte uint32 = 68 = 0x44). Any calldata appended beyond this 0x44 boundary is forwarded as extraData to the hook. In contrast, executeWithdrawals passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize. In the onExecuteWithdrawal hook (HooksConfig.sol), extraCalldataBytes is computed as `sub(calldatasize(), baseCalldataSize)`. Since calldatasize() == msg.data.length at the EVM level, this always evaluates to 0, meaning zero bytes of extra data are forwarded to the hook. This is acknowledged by the developer comment 'Use calldatasize() for baseCalldataSize to indicate no data should be passed as extraData', but this creates a security bypass: if a hooks contract uses extraData in onExecuteWithdrawal for authorization purposes (which the protocol explicitly supports via the extraData pattern across all hooks), the batch version completely bypasses this check. An attacker can simply call executeWithdrawals instead of executeWithdrawal to avoid providing any authorization data to the hook.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence from two source files confirms the inconsistency. In WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol, executeWithdrawal uses _runtimeConstant(0x44) while executeWithdrawals uses msg.data.length. In HooksConfig.sol, the onExecuteWithdrawal assembly computes extraCalldataBytes as sub(calldatasize(), baseCalldataSize), which yields 0 when baseCalldataSize equals msg.data.length.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This single-withdrawal function correctly passes 0x44 as baseCalldataSize (4-byte selector + 32-byte address + 32-byte uint32), allowing any appended calldata to be forwarded as extraData to the hook.

This batch function passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize, which equals calldatasize() at the EVM level. This forces extraCalldataBytes to 0 in onExecuteWithdrawal, stripping all extra data and bypassing any hook authorization that relies on extraData.

The hook computes extraCalldataBytes as calldatasize() - baseCalldataSize. When baseCalldataSize is msg.data.length (from batch path), this is always 0, so no extraData is ever forwarded to the hooks contract.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls executeWithdrawals([targetAddress], [targetExpiry]) instead of executeWithdrawal(targetAddress, targetExpiry)
2. executeWithdrawals passes msg.data.length as baseCalldataSize to _executeWithdrawal
5. Hook receives empty extraData, bypassing any authorization check based on extraData
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from executeWithdrawals to _executeWithdrawal to hooks.onExecuteWithdrawal always results in zero extraData bytes being forwarded. This is because msg.data.length (used as baseCalldataSize) equals calldatasize() by definition. In contrast, the single executeWithdrawal path uses 0x44 as baseCalldataSize, allowing calldatasize() - 0x44 bytes to be forwarded as extraData. The inconsistency means that any hook that validates extraData in onExecuteWithdrawal (e.g., checking signatures, access tokens, or credential proofs) can be bypassed by using the batch function.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that are relevant to hook configuration. The defect exists at the code level regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is straightforward: (1) A market is deployed with useOnExecuteWithdrawal enabled, and the hook contract uses extraData for authorization. (2) A caller who cannot provide valid authorization data calls executeWithdrawals([targetAccount], [targetExpiry]) instead of executeWithdrawal(targetAccount, targetExpiry). (3) The hook receives empty extraData and either does not check it (thinking it was already validated) or the authorization check passes vacuously for empty data. (4) The withdrawal executes without proper authorization. The attack requires only gas costs and a single transaction.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact depends on whether deployed hooks use extraData in onExecuteWithdrawal for access control. The protocol explicitly supports the extraData pattern across all hooks, making it a reasonable design choice for hook authors. If any hook relies on extraData for onExecuteWithdrawal authorization, the batch function provides a complete bypass. The defect is a code-level inconsistency between two public entry points performing the same operation, where one silently strips authorization data.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the executeWithdrawals batch function entirely if extraData support is important for onExecuteWithdrawal hooks
Add a per-element extraData parameter to executeWithdrawals (e.g., bytes[] calldata extraDatas) and compute appropriate baseCalldataSize for each element
If batch execution must not forward extraData, have the hook contract explicitly handle the case where extraData is empty and either reject or allow it based on the hook's security policy
Document the limitation clearly so hook authors are aware that onExecuteWithdrawal may be called with empty extraData via the batch path
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a hooks contract that reverts in onExecuteWithdrawal when extraData is empty (length 0). 2. Deploy a market with useOnExecuteWithdrawal enabled using that hooks contract. 3. Have a lender deposit, queue a withdrawal, and wait for expiry. 4. Call executeWithdrawal(lender, expiry) with appended extraData — should succeed. 5. Call executeWithdrawals([lender], [expiry]) — should bypass the extraData check since empty extraData is forwarded. 6. Alternatively, deploy a hook that logs extraData length and verify it's 0 for batch vs nonzero for single.
uncertainties: Whether any currently deployed hooks actually use extraData in onExecuteWithdrawal for authorization — the defect exists in the code regardless but practical impact depends on hook implementations

Whether the developer comment 'Use calldatasize() for baseCalldataSize to indicate no data should be passed as extraData' represents a deliberate design decision that was security-reviewed or an oversight about the implications
additional_testing_needed: Review all available hooks template implementations for extraData usage in onExecuteWithdrawal
Verify whether any hook base class or template enforces non-empty extraData for onExecuteWithdrawal
Test both executeWithdrawal and executeWithdrawals with a hook that validates extraData to confirm the bypass
